sections.content: Modulamp was the first time I had to think about a product as a business — financial modeling, market analysis, sustainability positioning. Useful. But entirely on paper, with no real users, no supplier conversations, no willingness-to-pay data. Just a well-structured hypothesis dressed up as a strategy. Exomembra made that gap concrete. Co-founding a startup meant the business questions couldn't stay theoretical. We assumed online visibility would work; it didn't — our audience was too niche. Showing up in person at events like Castlefest and Elfia did more than anything we tried online, but it also took more time than a small team could sustain. What both projects showed is that the parts of business design I found easier — structuring a case, communicating a proposition — are also the parts that stay comfortable because they don't require much from the outside world. The harder parts do: finding customers, sustaining momentum, finding out where your assumptions were wrong. There's a connection here to something I care about more broadly. In my vision I describe the risk of techno-solutionism — assuming a well-designed plan is a validated one before it's been tested against reality. Modulamp was, in a way, a business-plan-solutionist project. That tendency is one I want to keep being honest about in any future work that moves from concept into something that has to function in the world.
